name: Embalse de Yesa — circular of Sos del Rey Católico
name_original: Embalse de Yesa — circular de Sos del Rey Católico
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Sos del Rey Católico traverse the diverse landscapes of the Pre-Pyrenees in Aragon, Spain. The region features mountainous terrain, including views towards the Sierra de Leyre and Sierra de Santo Domingo, and is characterized by low-traffic, mostly paved roads. Cyclists encounter varied elevations, from the historical landscapes around the Yesa Reservoir to the winding paths near natural gorges like Foz de Lumbier and Arbaiun. The area offers a tranquil environment with abundant nature, including pine forests and the banks of the Aragón River. Best road cycling routes around Sos Del Rey Católico The most popular road cycling route is Embalse de Yesa — circular of Sos del Rey Católico, a 50.3 miles (80.9 km) trail that takes 3 hours 53 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the historical landscape surrounding the Yesa Reservoir. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Yesa Reservoir and Sos del Rey Católico Loop, a difficult 51.2 miles (82.4 km) path. This route also features significant elevation gain around the Yesa Reservoir. Local road cyclists also love the Puerto de Sos and Castiliscar Loop, a 28.6 miles (46.0 km) trail leading through varied terrain with moderate elevation changes, often completed in about 2 hours 19 minutes. Road cycling around Sos Del Rey Católico is defined by mountainous terrain, the Yesa Reservoir, and natural gorges, often on low-traffic roads. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to very challenging routes. The routes in Sos Del Rey Católico are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 600 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Sos Del Rey Católico's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain and difficulty can I expect on road cycling routes around Sos del Rey Católico?
A: The region around Sos del Rey Católico offers diverse landscapes, from the historical areas surrounding the Yesa Reservoir to the winding paths near natural gorges like Foz de Lumbier and Arbaiun. You'll encounter varied elevations, with routes ranging from moderate to very challenging. Many roads are mostly paved and feature minimal traffic, providing a tranquil cycling experience.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, for experienced riders seeking a challenge, the area offers routes with significant elevation gain. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992890036/embalse-de-yesa-circular-of-sos-del-rey-catolico" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Yesa — circular of Sos del Rey Católico&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 50.3-mile route with over 1,000 meters of ascent. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e985017256/yesa-reservoir-and-sos-del-rey-catolico-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Yesa Reservoir and Sos del Rey Católico Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is 51.2 miles long and also features substantial climbing.

Q: Are there any easier road cycling routes suitable for beginners?
A: While many routes in the area are moderate to difficult, there are options with less extreme elevation changes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990790948/puerto-de-sos-and-castiliscar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Sos and Castiliscar Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 28.6-mile route, offering a good balance of scenic views and manageable climbs. Another moderate option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992888820/long-along-the-onsuella-river-round-trip-from-sos-del-rey-catolico" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Long along the Onsuella River — round trip from Sos del Rey Catolico&lt;/a&gt;, which is 37 miles long with moderate elevation.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while cycling around Sos del Rey Católico?
A: Cyclists can enjoy stunning natural features such as the Yesa Reservoir and the dramatic Foz de Lumbier and Arbaiun Gorges. The routes also offer views towards the Sierra de Leyre and Sierra de Santo Domingo. You might also spot the medieval &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5266483" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruesta Castle&lt;/a&gt; overlooking the Yesa Reservoir.

Q: What historical sites can I explore near the cycling routes?
A: Sos del Rey Católico itself is a declared National Heritage site, with a medieval village structure, a 12th-century castle, and the Palace of Sada, birthplace of King Ferdinand II. Other nearby historical highlights include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/934414" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Xavier&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5332085" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Uncastillo Historic Center&lt;/a&gt;, offering rich cultural experiences alongside your ride.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Sos del Rey Católico?
A: The region is ideal for road cycling due to its generally tranquil atmosphere and abundant nature. While specific seasonal recommendations aren't provided, the Pre-Pyrenees climate typically offers pleasant conditions in spring and autumn. The forests, predominantly pine with poplars and beech, display beautiful colors in autumn, adding to the scenic appeal.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many of the routes in the area are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992890036/embalse-de-yesa-circular-of-sos-del-rey-catolico" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Yesa — circular of Sos del Rey Católico&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990790948/puerto-de-sos-and-castiliscar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Sos and Castiliscar Loop&lt;/a&gt;, providing convenient options for exploring the region.

Q: How many road cycling routes are there in Sos del Rey Católico?
A: There are over 35 road cycling routes available around Sos del Rey Católico, offering a wide variety of distances and difficulty levels to suit different preferences and fitness levels.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Sos del Rey Católico?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the diverse landscapes, the low-traffic, mostly paved roads, and the tranquil environment that allows for an immersive experience in nature.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes for endurance training?
A: For those looking for longer rides, the region offers routes that extend over significant distances. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31852935" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Luesia Castle – San Bartolomé Reservoir loop from Sos del Rey Católico&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging 88.5-mile route with over 2,200 meters of elevation gain, perfect for endurance training.

Q: Is the area suitable for family-friendly road cycling?
A: While many routes feature significant elevation, the region's low-traffic roads and beautiful scenery can be appealing. For families, it's advisable to look for routes with less elevation or shorter distances. The area is more geared towards moderate to challenging rides, so families with younger children might need to seek out specific, flatter sections or consider alternative cycling paths.
tips: Despite its curious and striking name, this town is serious when it comes to history and places to visit. Here is the Palace of Sada where King Ferdinand II of Aragon, known as the "Catholic", was born, a place worth visiting to understand the history of Spain. It is also very beautiful to walk its streets and appreciate its stone houses and discover some corner with beautiful panoramic views.

Flanked by the Aragón River and a town on the Camino de Santiago pass for centuries, it is said that this town has a monument on every street and you can see for yourself. Among the sites, the convent of San Francisco de Asís and del Carmen and the palace of Ongay-Vallesantoro stand out among other historical sites from Roman times.
The road is quite busy, but still it deserves to be a highlight. At some point you can take a refreshing dip!

We come across a beautiful picture offered by the medieval castle of Ruesta on top of a stony hill, from where there are good views of the Yesa reservoir. It dates from the 11th century and was an important defensive enclave of the kingdom of Aragon during the Reconquest period. Currently, only two towers remain.
